item.title: Airports are in chaos. ICE always helps with that.
item.description: Immigration agents with little public trust and training add to stress of flying for Americans.

item.title: The dark cloud looming over baseball’s new golden age
item.description: Will a lockout trample the sport’s best moment in years?

item.title: The Mideast pushed out the Muslim Brotherhood. Here’s where it landed.
item.description: Western tolerance and complacency prove fertile ground for radicalization.

item.title: March Madness has a ‘predicting’ problem
item.description: Prediction markets use language of finance to mask expansion of sports betting.

item.title: Why my dad called home for a UConn hoops play-by-play before he died
item.description: His heart stopped in January, but it carried dreams of March.

item.title: Neither tattoos nor slurs are slowing this Senate candidate down
item.description: The controversial Democrat is still piquing Mainers’ interest by channeling their frustrations.

item.title: Bernie Sanders doubles down on his dumbest idea
item.description: A federal moratorium on new data centers would make AI more expensive for everyone.

item.title: The Trump administration’s First Amendment promise
item.description: The Justice Department settles a Biden-era case about social media censorship.

item.title: Elizabeth Warren tries to jam the House with a flawed housing bill
item.description: Limitations on build-to-rent homes would reduce supply, which is why many progressives oppose them.

item.title: Cartoon by Dana Summers
item.description: Dana Summers cartoon on Democrats, shutdown and Pennsylvania Sen. John Fetterman.

item.title: The reality of regulating electronic cigarettes
item.description: Electronic cigarettes, agency funding and airport security, in readers’ eyes.

item.title: Gas prices skyrocket as Iran war drags
item.description: An upcoming Washington Post Opinions show. Stay tuned.

item.title: Trump is right about polls — and the better bet
item.description: A phone call with the president on the genius — and promise — of prediction markets.

item.title: Trump’s $300 million question: Can money fix a midterm mess?
item.description: Team Trump’s usage of its big cash advantage is the great “known unknown” of this election cycle.

item.title: George Will’s 2026 Opening Day Quiz
item.description: The Post columnist is dealing from a mound of baseball history. Watch out for the curveballs.

item.title: She taught backyard swim lessons. One complaint shut them down.
item.description: Local governments should encourage home businesses instead of making them illegal.

item.title: How to make the Strait of Hormuz irrelevant
item.description: Building pipelines to relieve a global chokepoint is no pipe dream.

item.title: Olympians earn the IOC billions. Guess who it almost never pays.
item.description: The IOC generates billions in revenue off the backs of athletes who often must pay their own way.

item.title: Data centers are gobbling up a resource — but not the one you think
item.description: Expanding footprint of AI infrastructure creates a thirst for more than electricity.

item.title: Iran is already ramping up its next war. Guess who the ‘enemy’ is.
item.description: The Islamic regime is determined to extinguish any internal challenge to its rule.

item.title: Trump wants voting to be a ‘privilege.’ Here’s what that means.
item.description: The poll tax prevented Americans from voting. Republican plans today pursue the same goal.

item.title: Health care is life or death. How can Americans be rational about it?
item.description:
When UnitedHealthcare CEO Brian Thompson was killed on a Manhattan sidewalk in December 2024, the nation was stunned. But the act of violence exposed the fury simmering beneath America’s health care debate. For many, the system feels impossibly expensive, confusing and unfair — especially when compared to other countries.
Host Megan McArdle is joined by Dr. Ashish Jha — physician, public health expert and former White House covid-19 response coordinator — to unpack what’s really going on inside the U.S. health care system. They explore why costs are so high and what it would actually take to build a system that works better for everyone.

item.title: Slashing gas taxes is good politics but terrible policy
item.description: Artificially stoking demand drives prices up in the long run.

item.title: RFK Jr.’s hypocritical quackery
item.description: FDA scientists warn that some popular peptides are ineffective and potentially dangerous.

item.title: Industrial policy for dummies
item.description: The World Bank is not the most credible voice in this debate.

item.description: An upcoming Washington Post Opinions show. Stay tuned.

item.title: Sen. Rick Scott: Big government causes problems
item.description: Government overspending, national debt and Virginia redistricting, in readers’ eyes.
